title: The Dark Night of the Soul and Solar Plexus Emotions
meta_description: You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, or maybe scrolling through every scary forum post you can find, convinced you're losing you
body:
The Dark Night of the Soul and Solar Plexus Emotions

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at the ceiling, or maybe scrolling through every scary forum post you can find, convinced you're losing your mind. I've been there. I know that feeling. That icy grip in your gut, the one that tells you something is terribly, irrevocably wrong. That's what we're talking about. The dark night of the soul, and specifically, how it hits you right in the solar plexus. It's not some abstract concept; it's a very real, very physical experience.

First off, let's get something straight. What you're going through - it's not made up. You're not crazy. You're in a process, a brutal, messy, terrifying process. Think of it like this: your old self is dying. And death, even spiritual death, is rarely a gentle affair. It's violent, it's disorienting, and it leaves you feeling utterly stripped bare. You know that feeling, right? Like everything you thought was solid has just dissolved into thin air. Your identity, your beliefs, your sense of safety - all gone. It's like being adrift in a storm, with no land in sight. I've seen this happen to so many people, and I lived it myself. The loneliness, the fear, the sheer exhaustion. It's all part of the package.

And that's where the solar plexus comes in. It's not just a chakra, you know? It's a real place in your body, a nerve center, right above your navel. It's where you feel fear, anxiety, dread, shame, anger - all those gnawing, gut-wrenching emotions. During a dark night, this area becomes a storm front. It's where all the unexamined stuff, all the trauma, all the suppressed feelings, decide to make their grand, unwelcome entrance. You might feel a constant churning, a burning sensation, nausea, panic attacks that seem to come out of nowhere. Your stomach might be in knots for days, weeks, months. It's not just "stress." It's deeper than that. It's your system purging, cleaning house, whether you like it or not.

I remember lying awake, night after night, with this intense, raw fear in my stomach. It felt like a physical weight, pressing down on me, making it impossible to breathe deeply. I'd try everything - meditation, breathing exercises, even just screaming into a pillow. Sometimes it would help for a minute, but then it would come right back. It's relentless. You're probably experiencing something similar. It's your body reacting to the immense energetic and psychological shifts happening within you. It's your nervous system in overdrive, trying to make sense of a reality that no longer makes sense. Does that land? Because it's not just in your head; it's in your gut, it's in your bones.

What's Happening in Your Solar Plexus

So, why the solar plexus? Why there, specifically? Look, it's often called the "seat of emotions" for a reason. It's deeply connected to your sense of self, your personal power, your boundaries, your will. When you're going through a dark night, all of these things are being dismantled. Your old sense of self is being questioned, maybe even annihilated. Your personal power feels like it's been ripped away. Your boundaries are dissolving, leaving you feeling exposed and vulnerable. You feel powerless, right? Like you have no control over what's happening to you. That's exactly what this process does. It strips away the illusion of control so you can find a deeper, more authentic power.

The solar plexus is also tied to your adrenal glands, your digestive system - all those things that go haywire when you're under extreme stress. You might be experiencing digestive issues, changes in appetite, constant fatigue, or bursts of nervous energy that leave you drained. It's all connected. Your body is screaming for attention, for release. It's trying to process years, maybe decades, of suppressed emotions. All those times you swallowed your anger, ignored your fear, pushed down your sadness - they're coming up now. And they're coming up through your solar plexus, demanding to be felt, to be acknowledged, to be released. It's a purification, a fiery detox. And it hurts like hell.

The Energetic Purge

Here's the thing: when your spiritual energy - call it kundalini, call it whatever you want - starts moving, it doesn't just bypass all your unresolved stuff. Quite the opposite. It hits it head-on. It's like a high-pressure hose blasting through clogged pipes. And those clogs? They're your old traumas, your limiting beliefs, your emotional wounds. When that energy hits them, it stirs everything up. It forces it to the surface. It's not trying to hurt you; it's trying to clean you out. But the cleaning process is incredibly painful.

I'll tell you what, I thought I was going to explode sometimes. The pressure in my chest, in my stomach, was immense. It felt like I had swallowed a ball of pure anxiety. And it wasn't just mental; it was a physical sensation, a burning, a churning, a tightness that wouldn't let up. That's the energetic purge. That's your system trying to clear out all the old, stagnant energy that's been holding you back. It's terrifying because it feels like it's going to consume you. But it won't. You're stronger than you think. You're built to withstand this. You wouldn't be going through it if you weren't.

You might find yourself having flashbacks, reliving old painful memories, or feeling intense emotions that seem disproportionate to your current situation. That's the old stuff resurfacing. It's not happening to punish you; it's happening to give you a chance to finally process and release it. It's a chance to grieve what you haven't grieved, to rage about what you haven't raged about, to feel what you haven't felt. It's ugly, it's messy, but it's necessary. Know what I mean? You can't bypass this. You have to go through it.

Facing Your Shadows

This period is also about facing your shadow self. All those parts of you that you've rejected, denied, or hidden away - they're coming out to play. Your anger, your shame, your jealousy, your deepest fears. They're all getting a spotlight. And it's terrifying because you've spent your whole life trying to keep them hidden, even from yourself. But here's the thing: you can't heal what you don't acknowledge. This dark night is forcing you to look at every single corner of your being, even the ones you'd rather pretend don't exist.

I remember thinking, "I'm a good person, why am I feeling so much rage?" Or "I'm supposed to be spiritual, why am I so full of fear?" That's the ego clinging to its old identity. The dark night strips that away. It shows you all the messy, imperfect, human parts of yourself. And it's okay. It's more than okay; it's necessary. Because only when you accept all of yourself - the light and the shadow - can you become truly whole. It's a brutal initiation, but it's one that leads to incredible integration. You can't just pretend these parts don't exist. They're part of you. And the solar plexus is where they often manifest most intensely, as that burning, churning core of discomfort.

Surviving the Storm: Practical Tips

So, you're in the thick of it. What do you do? How do you survive when it feels like your insides are being ripped apart? First, breathe. Seriously. Deep, slow breaths. It's not a cure, but it's an anchor. When your nervous system is in overdrive, conscious breathing is one of the few things that can bring it back down, even a little. I used to just focus on my exhale, making it longer than my inhale. It signals to your body that you're safe, even when you feel anything but.

Second, don't fight it. This is probably the hardest advice to take. Your instinct is to resist, to push it away, to make it stop. But resistance only amplifies the pain. It's like trying to hold a beach ball underwater - the harder you push, the more force it exerts back. Instead, try to lean into it. Acknowledge the fear, the pain, the dread. Say to yourself, "Okay, I'm feeling this. This is happening." Don't judge it, don't try to intellectualize it. Just feel it. It's excruciating, I know. But it's the only way through. I spent so long trying to fix myself, trying to escape, and it only made things worse. The moment I started allowing it, truly allowing it, was the moment things slowly, glacially slowly, started to shift.

Third, find safe spaces and safe people. This is not the time to isolate yourself. Even if you don't want to talk, just being in the presence of someone who understands, or at least doesn't judge, can be a lifeline. You might need to reach out to a therapist who understands spiritual emergencies. Not every therapist does, so choose carefully. You're not looking for someone to "fix" you, but someone to hold space for you. Someone who can say, "Yes, this is real. You're not alone." That's huge. Reading books can help too, just to know you're not the only one. I found so much solace in books like The Dark Night of the Soul by St. John of the Cross (paid link), even though it's ancient, because it described exactly what I was feeling. Also, The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) was a lifesaver, giving context to the chaos. And if you suspect kundalini, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) offers invaluable insights into the physical sensations.

  • Grounding: Seriously, get your feet on the earth. Walk barefoot. Spend time in nature. Eat nourishing food. Avoid stimulants. Anything that helps you feel connected to your body and the physical world can help counteract the disorienting feeling of being unmoored.
  • Movement: Gentle movement, like walking, stretching, or yoga, can help move stagnant energy. Don't push yourself, just listen to your body.
  • Journaling: Get it out. All of it. The fear, the anger, the despair. Don't censor yourself. It's a way to externalize some of that internal pressure.
  • Creative Expression: If you can, draw, paint, sing, dance. Expressing what you're feeling through art can be incredibly cathartic, even if it's just scribbling furiously on a page.
  • Self-Compassion: This is probably the hardest one. You're going to feel like a mess. You're going to feel like you're failing. But you're not. You're going through something incredibly difficult. Be kind to yourself. Treat yourself like you would a terrified child. You wouldn't yell at a scared kid, right? So don't yell at yourself.

The Integration Process

So yeah, you're not going to be in this state forever. It feels like it, I know. But it's a process, and processes have an end. The dark night eventually gives way to dawn. But the "dawn" isn't just flipping a switch back to normal. It's an integration. It's about bringing all those shattered pieces of yourself back together, but in a new, more authentic way. You're not going back to who you were before. That person is gone. You're becoming someone new, someone who has faced their deepest fears and survived.

The solar plexus, once a storm center, slowly starts to calm down. The churning subsides. The burning fades. It doesn't mean you'll never feel anxiety again, but it won't be that pervasive, all-consuming dread. You'll start to feel a sense of inner strength, a quiet power that wasn't there before. Because you've faced the abyss and lived. You've looked at your shadows and embraced them. You've learned to trust yourself in the absolute chaos.

This integration takes time. It's not a sudden event. It's a gradual unfolding. You'll have good days and bad days. You'll still feel triggered sometimes. But you'll have new tools, new perspectives, and a deeper understanding of yourself. The fear won't own you anymore. You'll be able to observe it, to feel it, and to let it pass, without being completely overwhelmed. It's like learning to surf a giant wave instead of getting pummeled by it. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is a great resource for understanding this whole integration phase.

One of the biggest shifts during integration is a re-establishment of your personal power, but it's not the ego-driven power you might have sought before. It's a power rooted in authenticity, in self-acceptance, in a deep connection to something larger than yourself. You'll find your voice, your boundaries, your sense of purpose - but they'll come from a place of true knowing, not from fear or a need for external validation. That solar plexus area, which was once a source of terror, becomes a source of calm, centered strength. It's where you feel your gut instincts, your intuition, your inner compass. It becomes a place of wisdom, not just pain.

Finding Meaning in the Madness

Honestly, when you're in the middle of it, it's impossible to see any meaning. It just feels like senseless suffering. But truth is, there's always meaning, even if it's only clear in hindsight. This dark night, this purging of your solar plexus, it's preparing you for something new. It's clearing the path for a deeper connection, a more authentic way of being. You're being stripped down to your essence, so you can rebuild on a stronger foundation.

You'll emerge from this with an incredible amount of compassion - for yourself, and for others. Because you've walked through hell, you'll understand what it means to suffer. You'll have a resilience you didn't know you possessed. You'll have a clarity about what truly matters. All those superficial things that used to occupy your attention? They won't matter as much anymore. You'll be focused on truth, on connection, on living from your heart, not from your fears.

So, if you're reading this at 3 AM, feeling that familiar knot in your stomach, know this: you're not alone. You're not broken. You're in a deep process of transformation. It's brutal, it's terrifying, but you're going to get through it. Keep breathing. Keep feeling. Keep reaching out. One foot in front of the other. You've got this. I know you do, because I've been there, and I came out the other side. You will too.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
